subject: Pharmacology
front: Drugs requiring TDM due to low therapeutic index?
back: Aminoglycosides, Antiepileptics, Lithium, Theophylline, Digoxin, Cyclosporine, Tacrolimus, Tricyclic antidepressants

subject: Pharmacology
back: Converts Dopamine to Norepinephrine (inside vesicles in adrenergic neurons)

subject: Pharmacology
back: GABAA, Glutamate (NMDA, AMPA, Kainate), Nicotinic receptors, 5-HT3

subject: Pharmacology
front: Gi/o GPCR: MOA and which receptors?
back: Inhibitory — decreases cAMP (Gi), decreases Ca2+ (Go), opens K+ channels. Receptors: M2 heart, alpha2, H3, 5-HT1 (presynaptic autoreceptors)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Norepinephrine IV: DOC for which shocks?
back: DOC for cardiogenic, septic, and neurogenic shock (NGS). For NGS: add Atropine if bradycardia present

subject: Pharmacology
front: Epinephrine IM: DOC for anaphylactic shock — dose and dilution?
back: DOC for anaphylactic shock. IM route: 0.3-0.5mg (1:1000 dilution)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Adenosine for SVT: IV dose in adults and children?
back: Adults: 6mg IV rapid push. Children: 0.1 mg/kg. DOC for acute SVT/PSVT

subject: Pharmacology
front: DOC for ventricular fibrillation and tachycardia?
back: Amiodarone (DOC). Exception: Digoxin/ischemia-induced VT/VF — DOC is Lidocaine

subject: Pharmacology
front: Lidocaine: DOC for which specific arrhythmia?
back: Digoxin-induced and ischemia-induced VT/VF (NOT standard VT/VF where Amiodarone is DOC)

subject: Pharmacology
back: DOC for acute Torsades de pointes (Long QT syndrome — both congenital and acquired)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Nicardipine: DOC for hypertensive emergency — route?
back: IV Nicardipine is DOC for hypertensive emergency. Also used: oral for pre-op HTN in pheochromocytoma, IV for intra-op HTN

subject: Pharmacology
front: Clonidine: DOC for hypertensive urgency — route?
back: Oral Clonidine is DOC for hypertensive urgency (no end organ damage). Also: ACEi/ARB, CCB

subject: Pharmacology
front: Sublingual NTG: DOC for which condition?
back: DOC for ACUTE angina attack (sublingual nitroglycerin — fastest relief)

subject: Pharmacology
back: Blocks LATE inward Na+ channel — reduces Na+ overload in ischemia — improves diastolic relaxation. Add-on in stable angina

subject: Pharmacology
back: K+ channel opener — vasodilator. Also has nitrate-like component. Add-on in stable angina

subject: Pharmacology
back: Blocks Na+-K+ ATPase — increases intracellular Na+ — activates Na-Ca exchanger — increases Ca2+ — positive inotropy

subject: Pharmacology
front: Verapamil/Diltiazem: most important side effect for combination?
back: Constipation (Verapamil). AV block (both). C/i with beta-blockers — causes total conduction block and asystole

subject: Pharmacology
back: Pitavastatin > Rosuvastatin (both have ceiling effect). Both are DOC for maximum LDL reduction

subject: Pharmacology
back: IL-11 analog (interleukin-11). Use: chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia

subject: Pharmacology
back: LAMA (Long-Acting Muscarinic Antagonist) — OD dosing. DOC for COPD maintenance

subject: Pharmacology
back: SABA (Short-Acting Beta-2 Agonist) — reliever. Used for acute bronchospasm in asthma and COPD

subject: Pharmacology
back: Beta-3 agonist — relaxes bladder detrusor. DOC for overactive bladder/urge incontinence

subject: Pharmacology
front: Ephedrine vs Phenylephrine for spinal anaesthesia hypotension?
back: Decreased HR: Ephedrine DOC. Normal HR: Phenylephrine DOC. Ephedrine has both alpha and beta effects; Phenylephrine is pure alpha1

subject: Pharmacology
back: DOC for postural hypotension. Alpha1 agonist — increases peripheral vascular resistance

subject: Pharmacology
back: Longest acting beta-blocker. Landiolol is shortest acting (shorter than Esmolol)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Brimonidine: which patients is it dangerous for?
back: Alpha2 agonist — crosses BBB in neonates — causes APNEA. Also causes drowsiness and hypotension

subject: Pharmacology
front: Glycopyrrolate vs Atropine as pre-anaesthetic: key difference?
back: Glycopyrrolate does NOT cross BBB (no CNS effects, no tachycardia in CNS). DOC as pre-anaesthetic medication to prevent aspiration

subject: Pharmacology
back: H=Height (efficacy), E=Efficacy, L=Left shift (potency), P=Parallel graph (affinity). Used to read graded dose-response curves

subject: Pharmacology
front: Drugs metabolized by acetylation that ALL cause drug-induced SLE (mnemonic HIPS Dance)?
back: Hydralazine, Isoniazid, Procainamide, Sulfonamides, Dapsone — all can cause drug-induced SLE

subject: Pharmacology
front: Valproate: pregnancy category and defect caused?
back: Category D — teratogenic risk but benefit > risk. Causes neural tube defects (NTDs)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Thalidomide: pregnancy category and defect caused?
back: Category X — teratogenic and AVOIDED (risk > benefit). Causes phocomelia (flipper limbs)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Cevimeline: DOC for xerostomia — which syndrome?
back: DOC for xerostomia (dry mouth) in Sjogren syndrome / sicca syndrome (M3 agonist — increases salivation)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Pilocarpine and Cevimeline: use in Sjogren syndrome?
back: Both parasympathomimetics used for xerostomia. Cevimeline is DOC (more selective M3 agonist). Pilocarpine is alternative

subject: Pharmacology
back: Miotic agent (muscarinic agonist) — increases trabecular outflow. Used for glaucoma (like Pilocarpine)

subject: Pharmacology
back: IV Procainamide (Na+ channel blocker — blocks accessory pathway). NOT Digoxin/Verapamil/Adenosine (these accelerate conduction through accessory pathway)

subject: Pharmacology
back: Direct Renin Inhibitor (DRI) — blocks renin enzyme. Only drug in this class

subject: Pharmacology
front: Pravastatin: why NOT metabolized by CYP450?
back: Pravastatin is not metabolized by CYP450 — hence protease inhibitors (CYP3A4 inhibitors) do NOT cause Pravastatin toxicity. DOC when PIs cause dyslipidemia

subject: Pharmacology
back: IV route — used for EMERGENCY treatment of SIADH (3rd line). Oral Tolvaptan/Mozavaptan used for long-term management

subject: Pharmacology
front: Life Can End mnemonic: drugs for depression with suicidal tendency?
back: L=Lithium, C=Clozapine, E=ECT (Electroconvulsive therapy — BEST treatment for depression with suicidal tendency)

subject: Pharmacology
front: REST + Bed mnemonic for ALS drugs?
back: Riluzole (DOC), Edaravone (2nd line), Sodium phenylbutyrate-taurursodiol SPT (2nd line), Tofersen (SOD-1 only), + Baclofen (muscle relaxant)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Livedo reticularis: which Parkinson drug causes it?
back: Amantadine (NMDA blocker) causes livedo reticularis (mottled purplish skin discoloration). Also causes ankle edema

subject: Pharmacology
front: Topiramate in migraine prophylaxis: described as?
back: Topiramate is described as TOXIC — causes renal stones, angle closure glaucoma, metabolic acidosis, weight loss (but weight loss makes it useful in obesity)

subject: Pharmacology
back: Pethidine (DOC) and Tramadol are used for post-anaesthetic chills/shivering

subject: Pharmacology
front: ABCZ mnemonic: zero side effects antipsychotics?
back: Aripiprazole, Brexpiprazole, Cariprazine, Ziprasidone — all weight neutral, partial agonists at D2 and 5HT2. New: Xanomeline (M1/M4 agonist, no D2 action)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Lithium doses: prophylaxis of mania vs acute mania?
back: Prophylaxis of mania: 0.6-1 mEq/L. Acute mania: 1-1.5 mEq/L. Toxicity: >1.5 mEq/L. Dialysis: >4 mEq/L

subject: Pharmacology
back: DOC for VRE (Vancomycin-resistant Enterococci) along with Linezolid. Both are last-line drugs for MDR gram-positive organisms

subject: Pharmacology
front: Valganciclovir oral: DOC for which CMV infection?
back: Oral Valganciclovir is DOC for CMV RETINITIS. IV Ganciclovir is DOC for CMV PNEUMONIA. Both cause bone marrow suppression

subject: Pharmacology
back: Meglitinides — close K+ channels in beta cells — insulin release. Short-acting — taken with MEALS to control post-prandial hyperglycemia

subject: Pharmacology
back: MC = 0 (pure glucocorticoid — NO mineralocorticoid activity — NO effect on blood pressure). Same for Dexamethasone and Betamethasone

subject: Pharmacology
back: HLA-B5801 gene — test before starting Allopurinol in Asian populations. Same gene for Febuxostat SJS

subject: Pharmacology
front: Acute asthma attack management with ICS + Formoterol?
back: 1 pump of ICS + Formoterol. Then Salbutamol 5 min later (if taken separately). Salbutamol 1 min then repeat if symptoms not relieved

subject: Pharmacology
front: Dronabinol and Nabilone: drug class and side effects?
back: CBR1 (Cannabinoid Receptor 1) agonists — used for chemotherapy-induced nausea. S/e: hypotension and blood shot eyes

subject: Pharmacology
back: Stool softeners — LEAST effective laxative type. Increase water and fat penetration into fecal mass

subject: Pharmacology
back: Warfarin causes stippled epiphyseal calcification (chondrodysplasia punctata), mid-facial hypoplasia (flat nose), and CNS defects

subject: Pharmacology
front: NSTEMI: why is thrombolytic therapy contraindicated?
back: C/i in NSTEMI — risk of bleeding exceeds benefit (thrombolytics used ONLY in STEMI where benefit outweighs risk)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Everolimus and Sirolimus: line of use post-transplant?
back: 2nd LINE drugs post-transplantation (mTOR inhibitors). 1st line: Calcineurin inhibitors (Cyclosporine/Tacrolimus)

subject: Pharmacology
front: Letrozole: when is it used in breast cancer?
back: DOC for POST-MENOPAUSAL ER+ve breast cancer (aromatase inhibitor — blocks peripheral estrogen synthesis). Tamoxifen for pre-menopausal
